subject: mathematics
title: Linear Programming
narrative: This rationalised NCERT chapter develops linear programming problems (LPP) and their solution by the graphical Corner Point Method only. Nodes build from the optimisation idea and the formulation vocabulary (objective function, decision variables, constraints, non-negative restrictions) through the feasible region and its solutions, the bounded/unbounded distinction and the two fundamental theorems, to the capstone Corner Point Method that solves an LPP. Every node carries a clean verbatim source statement.

nodes.title: Recognise an optimisation problem
nodes.brief: Identify an optimisation problem as one that seeks to maximise or minimise a quantity such as profit or cost.
nodes.quote: Such type of problems which seek to maximise (or, minimise) profit (or, cost) form a general class of problems called
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Spot the quantity being optimised.
An optimisation problem seeks to make some quantity as large or as small as possible. That quantity might be profit, cost, area, time, or use of resources — the hallmark is the word "maximise" or "minimise." For example, a furniture dealer wants to maximise his total profit from selling tables and chairs.

PART 2 — Recognise optimisation as a class of problems.
Problems that seek to maximise (or minimise) profit (or cost) form a general class called optimisation problems. An optimisation problem may involve finding maximum profit, minimum cost, or minimum use of resources. Linear programming problems are a very important special class of optimisation problems.
keyMoves: Spot the quantity to be made as large or as small as possible (profit, cost, resources)
Recognise this maximise/minimise goal as the hallmark of an optimisation problem
misconceptions: Thinking optimisation only ever means maximising (it also covers minimising)
Confusing the quantity to optimise with the limits placed on the choices

nodes.title: Write the objective function
nodes.brief: Form the linear objective function Z = ax + by that must be maximised or minimised.
nodes.quote: Linear function Z = ax + by, where *a*, *b* are constants, which has to be maximised or minimized is called a linear **objective function**.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the quantity to optimise and express it linearly.
The objective function is a linear function Z = ax + by, where a and b are constants, that has to be maximised or minimised. "Linear" means it has no x^2, xy, or other non-linear terms — just constant multiples of the decision variables added together.

PART 2 — Label the function Z.
In the furniture example, total profit = 250x + 75y, so Z = 250x + 75y is the objective function. It encodes exactly the quantity the optimisation problem tries to make as large (or small) as possible. Always write it in the form Z = ax + by and name it the objective function.
keyMoves: Identify the quantity to optimise (e.g. total profit or cost)
Express it as a linear function Z = ax + by with constant coefficients
misconceptions: Including non-linear terms (like xy or x^2) in the objective function
Confusing the objective function with a constraint inequality

nodes.title: Identify the decision variables
nodes.brief: Recognise the variables x and y in the objective function as the decision variables.
nodes.quote: Variables x and y are called **decision variables**.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Read off the unknowns the problem must choose.
The variables x and y appearing in the objective function are the quantities the problem must decide on. In the furniture example x = number of tables and y = number of chairs to buy. Their values are unknown until we solve the LPP — hence they are called decision variables.

PART 2 — Note the non-negativity constraint on decision variables.
Decision variables represent real-world quantities (items bought, hours worked), so they cannot be negative. This is recorded as x >= 0, y >= 0 and is always included. Choosing specific values for x and y determines the value of the objective function Z.
keyMoves: Read off the unknown quantities the problem must choose
Recognise these as the decision variables, always restricted to be non-negative
misconceptions: Treating the constants a, b in Z = ax + by as the decision variables
Forgetting that the decision variables are restricted to be non-negative

nodes.title: Write the linear constraints
nodes.brief: Express the restrictions on the variables as linear inequalities called constraints.
nodes.quote: The linear inequalities or equations or restrictions on the variables of a linear programming problem are called **constraints**.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Translate each real limit into a linear inequality.
Every practical restriction on the decision variables becomes a constraint. A limited budget of Rs 50,000 with tables at Rs 2500 and chairs at Rs 500 gives 2500x + 500y <= 50000, simplified to 5x + y <= 100. A storage limit of 60 pieces gives x + y <= 60. Each constraint is a linear inequality or equation in x and y.

PART 2 — Collect the constraints as a system.
All the constraints together restrict which combinations of (x, y) are allowed. They must all be linear — no x^2, no xy. In an LPP, the constraints and the non-negativity conditions x >= 0, y >= 0 together define the set of allowed decisions.
keyMoves: Translate each real limit (money, storage, time) into a linear inequality in x and y
Collect these inequalities as the constraints of the problem, keeping all relations linear
misconceptions: Writing a constraint as an equation when the limit is really an inequality (or vice versa)
Confusing the constraints with the objective function

nodes.title: Back-infer a missing constraint from a graphed feasible region
nodes.brief: Read the boundary line from the graph, write its equation, then decide ≤ or ≥ by checking whether a test point (usually the origin) is inside or outside the feasible region.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Read the boundary line from the graph.
A missing constraint defines a boundary line that cuts off part of the plane. From the graph, read two points that the boundary line passes through (these are typically on the axes or at labelled corners). Write the line equation. Example: the boundary passes through (1, 0) and (0, 2). Slope = (2−0)/(0−1) = −2, so the line is y = −2x + 2, i.e. 2x + y = 2.

PART 2 — Decide ≤ or ≥ by testing the origin.
The boundary line divides the plane into two half-planes. Test the origin (0, 0): substitute x = 0, y = 0 into 2x + y. We get 0. Is the origin INSIDE the feasible region? If yes, the constraint is 2x + y ≤ 2 (origin satisfies ≤). If the origin is OUTSIDE (excluded from the feasible region, as happens when the shaded region is above the line), the constraint is 2x + y ≥ 2. In the board question, the feasible region excludes the origin, so the answer is 2x + y ≥ 2.

PART 3 — Verify by checking a corner point of the feasible region.
Once you have a candidate constraint, substitute a known corner point of the feasible region and confirm it satisfies the inequality. If the corner (1, 0) is on the boundary and (2, 0) is inside the region, then 2(2) + 0 = 4 ≥ 2 ✓. This confirms the direction of the inequality.
keyMoves: Identify the two axis-intercept points of the boundary line and write the line equation
Test the origin: if the origin is inside the feasible region use ≤, if outside use ≥
Verify by substituting a known corner point of the feasible region into the candidate constraint
misconceptions: Assuming the constraint is ≤ just because constraints 'usually' restrict upward — always test a point
Reading the line equation wrong when the intercepts are not at integer coordinates
Forgetting that the boundary line itself belongs to the feasible region (use ≤ or ≥, not strict < or >)

nodes.title: Apply the non-negative restrictions
nodes.brief: Recognise the conditions x >= 0, y >= 0 as the non-negative restrictions on the decision variables.
nodes.quote: The conditions x >= 0, y >= 0 are called non-negative restrictions.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Add x >= 0 and y >= 0 to every LPP.
The conditions x >= 0 and y >= 0 are called non-negative restrictions. They must be included in every linear programming problem because the decision variables represent physical quantities (like numbers of items) that cannot be negative. They are always listed alongside the other constraints.

PART 2 — Recognise their geometric effect.
Graphically, x >= 0 confines the solution to the right half-plane (right of the y-axis) and y >= 0 confines it to the upper half-plane (above the x-axis). Together they restrict the feasible region to the first quadrant, where both coordinates are non-negative.
keyMoves: Add x >= 0 and y >= 0 to every LPP as the non-negative restrictions
Recognise that they confine the feasible region to the first quadrant
misconceptions: Forgetting to include x >= 0, y >= 0 among the constraints
Thinking the non-negative restrictions are optional extras rather than part of the problem

nodes.title: Define a linear programming problem
nodes.brief: State an LPP as finding the optimal value of a linear objective function subject to linear constraints and non-negativity.
nodes.quote: subject to the conditions that the variables are **non-negative** and satisfy a set of linear inequalities (called **linear constraints**).
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — State the structure of an LPP.
A Linear Programming Problem (LPP) is one concerned with finding the optimal value (maximum or minimum) of a linear objective function Z = ax + by, subject to the conditions that the variables are non-negative and satisfy a set of linear constraints (linear inequalities). The word "linear" means all relations used are linear; "programming" means determining a plan of action.

PART 2 — Write the standard form.
The standard form is: Maximise (or Minimise) Z = ax + by, subject to the linear constraints and x >= 0, y >= 0. For the furniture problem: Maximise Z = 250x + 75y subject to 5x + y <= 100, x + y <= 60, x >= 0, y >= 0. All three pieces — the objective function, the constraints, and the non-negativity conditions — are required to state the LPP fully.
keyMoves: State the objective function to be optimised
List the linear constraints and add the non-negativity conditions to complete the LPP
misconceptions: Calling a problem an LPP when the objective or a constraint is non-linear
Omitting the non-negativity conditions from the formulation

nodes.title: Identify the feasible region
nodes.brief: Recognise the feasible region as the common region determined by all the constraints including non-negativity.
nodes.quote: The common region determined by all the constraints including non-negative constraints
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Graph each constraint as a half-plane.
Each linear inequality defines a half-plane (one side of a straight line, including the line itself if the inequality is <= or >=). To find which side: substitute a test point (usually the origin) and check whether the inequality holds. Shade the side where it holds.

PART 2 — Take the common region of all half-planes.
The feasible region (also called the solution region) is the intersection of all the half-planes determined by the constraints, including x >= 0 and y >= 0. In the furniture problem it is the region OABC bounded by the two constraint lines and the axes. Every point inside this region satisfies all the constraints simultaneously.

PART 3 — Identify infeasible points.
Any point that lies outside the feasible region is an infeasible solution — it violates at least one constraint. For example, (25, 40) is infeasible in the furniture problem because it fails the storage constraint x + y <= 60.
keyMoves: Graph each constraint as a half-plane
Take the region common to all half-planes (including x >= 0, y >= 0)
Identify points outside this region as infeasible
misconceptions: Shading the region of a single constraint instead of the common region of all
Forgetting the non-negative constraints when finding the common region

nodes.title: Recognise feasible and infeasible solutions
nodes.brief: Identify points within and on the boundary of the feasible region as feasible solutions, others as infeasible.
nodes.quote: Points within and on the boundary of the feasible region represent feasible solutions of the constraints.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Check whether a point is feasible.
A point (x, y) is a feasible solution if it lies within or on the boundary of the feasible region — that is, if it satisfies every constraint simultaneously. Boundary points count as feasible. For example (10, 50), (0, 60) and (20, 0) are all feasible solutions of the furniture problem.

PART 2 — Classify an infeasible point.
Any point outside the feasible region is an infeasible solution because it violates at least one constraint. For instance, (25, 40) is infeasible for the furniture problem: x + y = 65 > 60 violates the storage constraint. Always check every constraint, not just one.
keyMoves: Check whether a point lies within or on the boundary of the feasible region
If so call it feasible; if it lies outside (violates any constraint) call it infeasible
misconceptions: Excluding boundary points from the feasible solutions (the boundary is included)
Calling a point feasible without checking every constraint

nodes.title: Recognise an optimal solution
nodes.brief: Identify an optimal solution as a feasible point that gives the optimal (max or min) value of the objective function.
nodes.quote: Any point in the feasible region that gives the optimal value (maximum or minimum) of the objective function is called an **optimal solution**.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Restrict attention to feasible points.
Only feasible solutions are candidates for the optimal solution; a point outside the feasible region is not eligible regardless of the Z value it produces. Among all feasible points, we seek the one (or ones) that give the best value of Z.

PART 2 — Identify the optimal solution by its Z value.
An optimal solution is a feasible point that gives the maximum or minimum value of the objective function. In the furniture problem, (10, 50) gives Z = 250(10) + 75(50) = 2500 + 3750 = 6250, which is the maximum profit; so (10, 50) is the optimal solution. There may be more than one optimal solution if two points give the same best Z value.
keyMoves: Restrict attention to feasible points only
Find the feasible point(s) giving the largest (or smallest) value of Z and call them optimal solutions
misconceptions: Looking for the optimal value outside the feasible region
Assuming an optimal solution must be unique

nodes.title: Decide if a feasible region is bounded or unbounded
nodes.brief: Classify a feasible region as bounded if it can be enclosed within a circle, otherwise unbounded.
nodes.quote: A feasible region of a system of linear inequalities is said to be bounded if it can be enclosed within a circle. Otherwise, it is called unbounded.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Check whether the region can be enclosed in a circle.
A feasible region is bounded if the entire region can be enclosed within some circle (however large). This is equivalent to saying the region is a finite polygon — it does not extend to infinity in any direction. For example, the furniture problem's region OABC is a finite quadrilateral, so it is bounded.

PART 2 — Recognise an unbounded region.
If the feasible region extends indefinitely in at least one direction — if some portion of it goes off to infinity — it is unbounded. This happens when some constraints are >= inequalities with no upper bound cutting the region off. For example, if the only constraints are x + y >= 10 and x, y >= 0, the region extends to infinity.

PART 3 — Understand the consequence for the optimum.
For a bounded region, the objective function always attains both a maximum and a minimum. For an unbounded region, a maximum or minimum of Z may not exist; but if it does exist, Theorem 1 guarantees it occurs at a corner point.
keyMoves: Sketch the feasible region
Check whether some circle can enclose the whole region (bounded) or it extends to infinity (unbounded)
Note that boundedness guarantees both a max and a min exist
misconceptions: Calling a large but finite region unbounded just because it looks big
Assuming an unbounded region always lacks an optimum (it may still have one)

nodes.title: Find the corner points of the feasible region
nodes.brief: Identify corner points (vertices) as intersections of two boundary lines of the feasible region.
nodes.quote: A corner point of a feasible region is a point in the region which is the intersection of two boundary lines.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Locate where boundary lines of the feasible region intersect.
A corner point (vertex) of a feasible region is a point in the region where two boundary lines meet. Boundary lines come from the constraint equations (replace <= with =). For the furniture problem, the boundary lines are 5x + y = 100, x + y = 60, x = 0 and y = 0.

PART 2 — Find the coordinates by solving pairs of equations.
To find a corner point, solve the pair of equations of the two boundary lines meeting there. For example, solving 5x + y = 100 and x + y = 60 simultaneously: subtracting gives 4x = 40, so x = 10, y = 50. The four corner points of the furniture problem are O(0,0), A(20,0), B(10,50) and C(0,60).
keyMoves: Identify the boundary lines of the feasible region
Solve each pair of intersecting boundary lines simultaneously to find the corner point coordinates
misconceptions: Taking an interior point as a corner point
Missing a vertex by not intersecting every relevant pair of boundary lines

refreshers.trigger: Student must turn the constraints into boundary lines and pick the two lines meeting at a corner (e.g. from 5x + y <= 100, x + y <= 60, x >= 0, y >= 0) but does not know how to get the line equations or which pair to solve.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: quick one before we hunt for corners: a corner is where two BOUNDARY lines cross - so what are the actual line equations hiding inside constraints like 5x + y <= 100, x + y <= 60, and the conditions x >= 0, y >= 0?
ladder: Take one inequality, say 5x + y <= 100. The edge of that half-plane is a straight line - what equation do you get if you replace the <= with an = ?
Do the same for x + y <= 60: its boundary line is x + y = 60. So each constraint hands you one boundary line.
The conditions x >= 0 and y >= 0 are boundary lines too - which familiar lines are x = 0 and y = 0?

A corner is where TWO of these boundary lines cross. To find corner B you pair the two that meet there, e.g. 5x + y = 100 with x + y = 60; for a corner on an axis you pair a constraint line with x = 0 or y = 0.
refreshers.answer: Every constraint inequality has a boundary line: replace the <= (or >=) with = to get it, so 5x + y <= 100 gives the line 5x + y = 100 and x + y <= 60 gives x + y = 60. The non-negativity conditions are boundary lines as well - x = 0 is the y-axis and y = 0 is the x-axis. A corner point sits where two of these lines cross, so to find a particular vertex you choose the two boundary lines that meet there and solve that pair together. In this node that is exactly how you get coordinates like B(10,50) - by pairing 5x + y = 100 with x + y = 60 - and corners like A(20,0) or C(0,60) by pairing a constraint line with y = 0 or x = 0.
refreshers.returnCue: Now that you can read off the boundary lines and pick the right pair, solve each pair to get the corner coordinates and list all the vertices of the feasible region.

nodes.title: Apply Theorem 1: optimum at a corner point
nodes.brief: Use Theorem 1: when Z has an optimal value it must occur at a corner point of the feasible region.
nodes.quote: this optimal value must occur at a corner point
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — State Theorem 1.
Theorem 1: Let R be the feasible region (convex polygon) for an LPP and Z = ax + by be the objective function. When Z has an optimal value (maximum or minimum), this optimal value must occur at a corner point (vertex) of the feasible region. The proof is beyond the scope of this chapter.

PART 2 — Use Theorem 1 to reduce the search.
Because the feasible region has only finitely many corner points, Theorem 1 reduces finding the optimum from searching infinitely many feasible points to checking finitely many vertices. If Z is to be maximised, compute Z at each vertex, take the largest value — that is the maximum. Theorem 1 only locates the optimum when one exists; it does not guarantee existence.
keyMoves: Recall that the feasible region has finitely many corner points
Invoke Theorem 1: any optimal value of Z occurs at a corner point, so restrict the search to vertices
misconceptions: Searching the interior of the region for the optimum
Forgetting that Theorem 1 only locates the optimum WHEN one exists, not that one always exists

nodes.title: Apply Theorem 2: bounded region has both max and min
nodes.brief: Use Theorem 2: if R is bounded, Z has both a maximum and a minimum, each at a corner point.
nodes.quote: If R is **bounded**, then the objective function Z has both a **maximum** and a **minimum** value on R and each of these occurs at a corner point
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — State Theorem 2.
Theorem 2: Let R be the feasible region for an LPP and Z = ax + by the objective function. If R is bounded, then Z has both a maximum and a minimum value on R, and each of these occurs at a corner point (vertex) of R. This theorem guarantees existence, not just location.

PART 2 — Apply it: just compare Z at the vertices.
When the feasible region is bounded, you can find both the maximum and minimum of Z simply by evaluating Z at each corner point and reading off the largest (M) and smallest (m) values. No further check is needed. In Example 2 of NCERT, with bounded region ABC, Z = 200x + 500y at A(0,5) = 2500, at B(4,3) = 2300, at C(0,6) = 3000; so minimum = 2300 at B(4,3).

PART 3 — Handle the unbounded case.
If the feasible region is unbounded, M or m might not be the actual maximum or minimum. An additional open-half-plane check (Step 3(ii) of the Corner Point Method) is needed to confirm.
keyMoves: Check whether the feasible region is bounded
If bounded, conclude Z attains both a max and a min, each at a vertex, and just compare vertex values
If unbounded, use the open-half-plane check before claiming the extremum
misconceptions: Assuming an unbounded region always has both a max and a min
Concluding the smallest vertex value is the minimum without checking boundedness

nodes.title: Recognise multiple optimal solutions
nodes.brief: Detect that when two corner points give the same optimal value, every point on the segment joining them is also optimal.
nodes.quote: every point on the line segment joining these points will also give the same maximum (or minimum) value.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Detect when two corner points share the same optimal value.
While evaluating Z at the corner points, if two distinct vertices produce the same optimal value (both maximum or both minimum), the problem has multiple optimal solutions. For example in NCERT Example 3, Z = 3x + 9y gives Z = 180 at both C(15,15) and D(0,20).

PART 2 — Conclude that every point on the joining segment is also optimal.
If two corner points C and D both give the same optimal value of Z, then every point on the line segment CD also gives that same value. This is because Z is linear, so it varies linearly (in fact constantly) along the segment between two points where it takes the same value. Report the solution as: the optimum is 180 attained at every point of segment CD.
keyMoves: Notice when two distinct corner points share the same optimal Z value
Conclude every point on the segment joining them is also optimal, and report multiple solutions
misconceptions: Reporting only one corner point when the optimum is attained at two
Thinking the shared value differs along the joining segment (it stays the same)

nodes.title: Solve an LPP by the Corner Point Method
nodes.brief: Solve a linear programming problem graphically by evaluating Z at the corner points of the feasible region.
nodes.quote: Evaluate the objective function Z = ax + by at each corner point.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Find the feasible region and its corner points.
First, graph all the constraints (including x >= 0, y >= 0) to identify the feasible region. Then find the corner points (vertices) either by inspection or by solving each pair of intersecting boundary lines simultaneously. For example, for Maximise Z = 4x + y subject to x + y <= 50, 3x + y <= 90, x >= 0, y >= 0: the corner points are O(0,0), A(30,0), B(20,30) and C(0,50).

PART 2 — Evaluate Z at each corner point.
Compute the value of Z = ax + by at every corner point. Let M denote the largest value and m the smallest. For the above example: Z at O = 0, at A = 120, at B = 110, at C = 50; so M = 120 at A(30,0).

PART 3 — Read off the optimum using the bounded/unbounded rule.
If the feasible region is bounded, M is the maximum and m is the minimum of Z. If unbounded, check whether the open half-plane ax + by > M (for maximum) or ax + by < m (for minimum) has any common point with the feasible region; if not, M or m is the optimum; if yes, no finite optimum exists. Report the optimal solution and the optimal value.
keyMoves: Find the feasible region and its corner points
Evaluate Z = ax + by at every corner point and identify M (largest) and m (smallest)
Apply the bounded/unbounded rule to read off the optimum, then report the optimal point(s)
misconceptions: Reading off the optimum from an interior point instead of the vertices
Treating the largest vertex value as the maximum on an unbounded region without the half-plane check
